purpose: Provides more honest uncertainty communication by separately rating confidence in the sign/direction of an effect versus its magnitude
components: Sign confidence: probability the directional effect is correct (e.g., 7/10 for aging depresses r*)
Magnitude confidence: probability the quantified estimate is accurate (e.g., 4/10 for the 1.0-1.5pp estimate)
Explicitly distinguishes between knowing the direction and knowing the size
Direction/sign confidence: based on qualitative mechanism and directional evidence
Magnitude confidence: based on statistical power, sample size, and effect size identification
Application protocol: all demographic-to-financial claims should carry dual confidence ratings
Examples: fiscal deterioration (9/10 direction, 5/10 magnitude), aging-r* (7/10 sign, 4/10 magnitude), China (9/10 demographics, 4/10 markets)
current_assessment: Validated by pair_2 as 'genuinely useful and should become standard practice.' Applied throughout the synthesis. Should be formalized for all future KB entries.
limitations: Requires sufficient evidence to assess even sign confidence
Does not address timing uncertainty as a separate dimension
May create false precision in the confidence numbers themselves
Does not address timing uncertainty (a third dimension)
May create false precision in the split between direction and magnitude
Requires subjective judgment on where to draw the direction/magnitude boundary
